literal: transformation, real change-into-another-form; from pari- + √nam 'to bend'
invariant_definition: Real transformation — the substrate genuinely changes into the new form (as milk into curd). Sāṅkhya is the paradigm *pariṇāma-vādin*: *prakṛti* really evolves into the manifest world. In Vedānta, *pariṇāma-vāda* is the position of Bhāskara, Viśiṣṭādvaita (with respect to *acit*), Dvaita (Viṣṇu causes real transformations of *prakṛti*), and Acintya-Bhedābheda. Advaita rejects *pariṇāma-vāda* in favor of *vivarta-vāda*. Vallabha modifies it as *avikṛta-pariṇāma* — transformation without modification of essence.

per_school.definition: *Pariṇāma-vāda* in the strong sense: *brahman* really transforms (under *upādhi*) into the world. The world is *brahman*'s real product, not its appearance.

*Pariṇāma* applies to *acit* (matter) — *prakṛti* really evolves into the manifest world under the Lord's will. *Brahman*-essential nature does not undergo *pariṇāma*; it remains immutable as *cit*-essence.
Real *pariṇāma* of *prakṛti* under Viṣṇu's causal will. *Brahman* (Viṣṇu) does not undergo modification; *prakṛti* does.
*Pariṇāma-vāda* is rejected: *brahman* cannot really transform, since it is partless and immutable. What looks like transformation is *vivarta*.
*Avikṛta-pariṇāma* — transformation without modification: a third position between *vivarta* and ordinary *pariṇāma*.

translator_note: 'Real transformation' is the standard gloss. The *pariṇāma*-*vivarta* opposition is fundamental and should be flagged in English exposition.
